items.desc: 20-min skim. Direct mirror of MAE's 10 LLM judges: clinician-authored case rubrics matched human-human agreement at ~1000× lower cost. Take 3 bullets you can quote to Jun re: how to scale the LLM-judge slots without hiring more annotators.

sections.title: 📄 Paper of the day — Case-specific rubrics for LLM-judge eval

paper.title: Case-Specific Rubrics for Clinical AI Evaluation: Methodology, Validation, and LLM-Clinician Agreement Across 823 Encounters
paper.authors: Aaryan Shah, Andrew Hines, Alexia Downs, Denis Bajet, Paulius Mui, Fabiano Araujo
paper.tldr: 20 clinicians authored 1,646 case-specific rubrics across 823 clinical cases. An LLM-based scoring agent applied to those rubrics matched clinician-clinician ranking agreement (Kendall tau 0.42–0.46 vs 0.38–0.43) at ~1000× lower cost. The rubric methodology generalizes any time you want LLM-judge evaluation that tracks expert judgment.
paper.why: Direct mirror of MAE's 10 LLM-judge slots in the 29-evaluator suite. The case-specific-rubric pattern is the missing layer between rule evaluators (cheap, brittle) and human PPE runs (slow, expensive). Worth quoting to Jun: 'we can scale judge coverage 1000× by separating rubric-authoring (rare, expert) from rubric-applying (LLM, frequent).'
key_findings: Clinician-authored rubrics discriminated effectively between high/low quality outputs (median score gap 82.9%) with high scoring stability (median range 0.00%).

LLM-clinician ranking agreement (tau 0.42–0.46) matched or exceeded clinician-clinician agreement (tau 0.38–0.43) — i.e. the LLM judge is as reliable as a second human reviewer when given a good rubric.
Cost ~1000× lower than per-instance expert review enables far broader eval coverage — exactly the regime MAE needs to iterate on agent prompts daily.

sections.title: 🌐 What the world is reading (HN front page)

items.title: Microsoft and OpenAI end their exclusive and revenue-sharing deal
items.summary: Microsoft and OpenAI have wound down the exclusive cloud + revenue-share arrangement that defined their partnership. OpenAI is now free to deploy on other clouds; Microsoft retains some equity but no exclusivity.
items.why: Reshapes the model-platform layer you build on top of. Worth tracking because (a) ByteDance's own model strategy will be read against this backdrop in Q3 planning, and (b) any third-party LLM-judge infra you propose for MAE has a wider vendor menu now.

items.title: Microsoft VibeVoice: Open-source frontier voice AI
items.summary: Microsoft released an open-weights frontier voice model (TTS + voice cloning) under a permissive license, with checkpoints and inference code on GitHub.
items.why: Adjacent to your video-gen interest: voice is the next axis after visuals for AI editing pipelines. Useful prior art if MAE ever folds in audio synthesis evaluators — and a clean example of how 'frontier-grade open weights' is becoming table stakes.

items.title: 4TB of voice samples stolen from 40k AI contractors at Mercor
items.summary: Mercor — the AI training-data contractor marketplace — disclosed a breach exposing ~4TB of voice samples from ~40,000 contractors, raising synthetic-voice fraud concerns.
items.why: Cautionary signal for any eval pipeline that ingests human-labeled data: PII leakage scales with annotator count. If MAE ever pays human raters at scale, the data-handling story matters as much as the eval methodology — bring this up if Jerry/Jun discuss PPE expansion.
